description: Skill parity between installed OpenCode (~13 skills) and repo source (~2 skills) was explicitly deferred from Profile Parity closure scope. The repo's .opencode/skills/ directory contains only cloudflare and agents-sdk fixtures, whereas installed OpenCode ships with ~13 production skills including design, docker, github, kubernetes, postgres, python, react, terraform, and web. Full skill parity requires importing or reimplementing ~11 missing skills.
best_practice_anchor: Agent profile parity should include skill parity when the orchestrator agent delegates to skill-using subagents
recommended_slice: Audit installed skills vs repo skills, import or reimplement missing skills, validate subagent load and permission compatibility

description: DuckDB WASM in-process path is stubbed and returns null. The CLI subprocess path (duckdb CLI via exec/spawn) works but spawns one subprocess per query with no pooling. A WASM in-process path using @duckdb/duckdb-wasm would be more robust for local/desktop usage — lower latency, no CLI dependency, and better error handling.
best_practice_anchor: In-process database engines should prefer WASM/native bindings over CLI subprocess for latency and resource efficiency
recommended_slice: Implement WASM worker pool for DuckDB queries, provide fallback to CLI subprocess when WASM is unavailable

description: Coordination tables (coordination_claim, coordination_reservation, coordination_fan_out) are created via CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS at runtime in coordination.pg.sql.ts rather than being part of the generated migration-pg DDL. While this works for both Postgres and SQLite backends, it means the coordination schema is not captured in the canonical migration DDL. For production Postgres deployments that use managed migrations, these tables should ideally be in the generated migration-pg/ DDL.
best_practice_anchor: All production schema definitions should be captured in migration DDL, not created ad-hoc at runtime
recommended_slice: Add coordination tables to the drizzle-kit schema path so they are included in migration-pg/ DDL generation; remove CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS fallback

description: The DrizzleLikeClient wrapper in adapter.ts is an intentional architectural boundary that allows the Drizzle ORM query surface to work with both Postgres/PGlite and DuckDB backends through a unified interface. However, this abstraction adds maintenance cost: it must be kept in sync with Drizzle ORM upstream releases, the structural type assertions (as DrizzleLikeClient casts) bypass type safety, and the afterCommit delegation varies per backend (PGlite defers to query callback, DuckDB no-ops). The cost is acceptable for now but should be revisited when Drizzle ORM stabilizes its Effect integration.
best_practice_anchor: Bridge abstractions should be temporary; prefer upstream-native interfaces once available
recommended_slice: Monitor upstream drizzle-orm/effect-sqlite stabilization; migrate DrizzleLikeClient to upstream Effect SQL integration when available

why_matters: DatabaseAdapter.defaultLayer (adapter.ts:396) also uses Layer.unwrap to select PgAdapter vs SQLiteAdapter based on DatabaseConfig. While this one has its dependency (DatabaseConfig.defaultLayer) properly provided via outer Layer.provide, it shares the same pattern as the fixed InstanceLayer.layer. If the DatabaseConfig.Service is not resolved in time (e.g., if ConfigProvider is not set before layer construction), the runtime will fail to construct the adapter.
best_practice_anchor: Effect Layer.unwrap: Prefer static composition with Layer.effect + explicit provides over Layer.unwrap(Effect.promise(...)) to keep the dependency graph visible at compile time
recommended_slice: Refactor DatabaseAdapter.defaultLayer from Layer.unwrap to a Layer.effect that declares DatabaseConfig.Service as an explicit requirement with an outer Layer.provide. This makes the selection logic inspectable and prevents future runtime resolution failures.

why_matters: DuckDB-aware tools (record-stress-wave, out-of-scope-finding, etc.) can write to JSONL but not to DuckDB because no write-capable helper exists. DuckDB.Service is read-only and the pipeline's execDuckDB isn't exposed to tools.
recommended_slice: Create packages/opencode/src/storage/duckdb-pipe.ts with execDuckDBWrite helper, expose to tools via DuckDBConfig check

why_matters: Schema.String.annotations is not a function — tool-failure.ts crashes during import, blocking duckdb-query.test.ts and any other test that loads this module. This is a pre-existing defect that prevents full test coverage of the DuckDB query tool.
best_practice_anchor: Effect Schema API usage — Schema.String.annotations was likely changed or removed in a newer effect package version
recommended_slice: Fix tool-failure.ts Schema.String.annotations call — either use the correct API or use Schema.String + .pipe(Schema.annotations(...)) if the annotations method was moved

why_matters: Desktop Electron app has no main-process uncaughtException/unhandledRejection handler, no Content Security Policy, and disabled Windows update signature verification. These gaps can cause silent crashes, XSS vulnerabilities, and supply-chain attack vectors.
best_practice_anchor: Electron Security Guidelines — main process crash resilience, CSP requirement, code signing verification
recommended_slice: Mission: Secure the Electron shell — add global error handlers, CSP headers, enable update signature verification, add Linux publish targets.

why_matters: @opencode-ai/core uses wildcard export \"./*\": \"./src/*.ts\" with no public API boundary. Any internal refactor can silently break 476+ import sites across the monorepo. This blocks architectural evolution.
best_practice_anchor: Package architecture — explicit public API surface with curated subpath exports. No wildcard re-exports from internal modules.
recommended_slice: Mission: Create public API boundary for @opencode-ai/core — replace wildcard export with explicit barrel, identify stable surface, add lint rule against bare imports.

why_matters: Desktop package has only 2 test files for 29 source modules. No IPC handler tests, no window lifecycle tests, no server/sidecar tests. This is a shipping Electron desktop app with critical untested paths.
best_practice_anchor: Desktop application testing — IPC contracts, window lifecycle, sidecar management all require automated test coverage for a production app.
recommended_slice: Mission: Add comprehensive test infrastructure for Electron desktop — IPC handler contract tests, window lifecycle tests, sidecar health simulation tests.

why_matters: @opencode-ai/plugin (public Plugin SDK) and @opencode-ai/sdk (public JS API) have zero tests. Plugin authors and SDK consumers have no automated quality guarantees. This is a public API trust issue.
best_practice_anchor: SDK testing — public API packages must have test suites for initialization, contracts, error handling, and lifecycle.
recommended_slice: Mission: Add test suites for @opencode-ai/plugin (tool/TUI extension API) and @opencode-ai/sdk (client initialization, server lifecycle, error handling).

why_matters: Electron desktop IPC has compile-time TypeScript types only — no runtime schema validation at the preload boundary. A compromised renderer can send arbitrary IPC messages. Industry standard (VS Code, Slack) uses JSON Schema / Zod validation.
best_practice_anchor: Electron Security — runtime IPC schema validation at the preload boundary to guard against compromised renderer processes.
recommended_slice: Mission: Add runtime IPC message schema validation — validate all incoming IPC channel/payload against Effect Schema or Zod at the preload boundary.

why_matters: These files use Schema.String.annotations() which is not a valid method in the Effect Schema API used by this repo. The correct method is .annotate() (without 's'). All other tool files in the directory already use .annotate() correctly. These will fail typecheck or runtime.
best_practice_anchor: Effect Schema API — .annotate() is the correct method, not .annotations()
recommended_slice: Fix all remaining tool files using .annotations() — replace with .annotate() across the 10 affected files

why_matters: 10 tool files still use Schema.String.annotations({...}) which is a runtime error — the method doesn't exist in the current Effect Schema version. The correct API is Schema.String.annotate({...}). These files will crash when their tool is invoked.
best_practice_anchor: Effect Schema .annotate() API — .annotations() is not a function in the installed Effect version
recommended_slice: Batch-fix all 10 files: replace Schema.String.annotations({ with Schema.String.annotate({ across packages/opencode/src/tool/
